mnemonics: Please Excuse My Dear Aunt Sally (for Propositional logic: Parentheses, Exponents/Equivalences, NOT, AND, OR, IMPLIES, IFF)
Dom's Cat Eats Fish, Then Ignooses Birds (Laws: Domination, Complement, Identity, Idempotent, Commutative, Associative, Distributive, De Morgan's, Absorption, Negation, Implication, Biconditional)
keyPoints: Logical equivalence (p ≡ q) means two propositions always have the same truth value in all cases.
The laws of logic (e.g., Identity, Domination, Idempotent, Double Negation, Commutative, Associative, Distributive, De Morgan's, Absorption) are fundamental equivalence rules.
These laws allow for the simplification of complex logical expressions and proving equivalences without truth tables.
commonMistakes: Confusing logical equivalence (≡) with a biconditional (↔); equivalence is a meta-statement about two propositions, while a biconditional is a connective within the logic.
Misapplying De Morgan's Laws by incorrectly distributing the negation (e.g., ¬(p ∧ q) is ¬p ∨ ¬q, not ¬p ∧ ¬q).
Assuming the order of operations is the same as in arithmetic; negation (¬) has the highest precedence, followed by ∧, then ∨, then →, and finally ↔.
